summary:
The Kangxi Emperor (r. 1661–1722 AD) was the second Qing emperor to rule over China proper and the longest-reigning emperor in Chinese history (61 years), whose reign established the political, cultural, and territorial foundations of the [[event:2337|Qing Dynasty's]] golden age. Ascending the throne at age eight and taking personal control at fifteen, Kangxi proved a remarkably capable ruler: he suppressed the Revolt of the Three Feudatories (1673–1681), a massive rebellion by three powerful Chinese generals who had helped the Manchus conquer China, and incorporated Taiwan into the empire (1683) by defeating the forces of the [[event:3306|Koxinga family]]. He personally led military campaigns against the Dzungar Mongols in Central Asia and negotiated the [[event:2444|Treaty of Nerchinsk]] (1689) with Russia — China's first treaty with a European power, defining the Siberian border.

Kangxi was a genuine polymath who mastered Chinese classical learning, studied Western mathematics and sciences with Jesuit missionaries (whom he patronized at court while ultimately supporting restrictions on Christianity), composed poetry, practiced calligraphy, and went on regular inspection tours of the empire. His cultural projects were monumental: the Kangxi Dictionary (45,000 characters), the Peiwen Yunfu (a poetry encyclopaedia), and the Complete Prose of the Tang and Song — systematic efforts to codify Chinese literary heritage. His rule was also characterized by deliberate efforts to win Han Chinese acceptance of Qing rule through Confucian governance and support for Chinese institutions. The stability and prosperity of his reign laid the foundation for the 18th-century High Qing period when China's population doubled and territorial control reached its maximum extent.
